§1 Ordinary quadratic singularities (canonical forms)

In this §, we take up again the results of VI 6, adopting a « henselian » rather than « formal » point of view. We give canonical forms for ordinary quadratic singularities and their deformations, into which they can be put locally for the étale topology.

The results are an easy consequence of VI 6, of the implicit function theorem (in the form of Tougeron) and of the theorem of R. Elkik on versal henselian moduli of isolated singularities. At the time of the oral seminar, the latter was not yet available ; a weaker theorem, covering only the case (which sufficed for us) of complete intersections, had been proved and will not be published.

1.1.1 Let \(f:X\to S\) be a morphism, \(X\) being locally describable by \(p\) equations in the relative affine space \(\mathbb A_S^{n+p}\) of dimension \(n+p\) over \(S\). The jacobian ideal \(J^n(X/S)\) was defined in VI 5. If \(X\) is the subscheme of \(\mathbb A_S^{n+p}\) defined by the equations \(f_i=0\) \((1<i\leq p)\), it is the sheaf of ideals generated by the \(p\times p\) minors of the jacobian matrix \((\partial f_i/\partial x_j)\). The following theorem is well known. A particular case of its formal analogue is proved in Bourbaki Alg. Comm. III §4, n° 6, cor. 3 to Th. 2 ; the analogue \(C^\infty\) is proved in Tougeron (idéaux de fonctions différentiables — Thèse — mai 1967 — Rennes) ; the case considered here is treated in Artin, Algebraic approximation of structures over complete local rings, Publ. Math. IHES 36 (Zariski's volume) lemma 5.10.

1.1.2 Theorem (Tougeron-Artin). Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a henselian local ring \(A\), \(f:X\to S\) a morphism as in 1.1.1., \(a\) and \(I\) two ideals of \(A\) distinct from \(A\), and \(\mathcal J\) a sheaf of ideals of \(X\) contained in \(J^n(X/S)\). We assume \(I\) of finite type. Let \(s\) be a section of \(f\) above \(S/I\) : \(s\in X(A/I)\). Let us denote by \(s^*\mathcal J^2\) the ideal of \(A\) inverse image in \(A\) of the ideal \(s^*\mathcal J^2\) of \(A/I\).

If \((s^*\mathcal J^2).a\supset I\), then there exists a section \(s_1\) of \(f\) : \(s_1\in X(A)\) congruent to \(s\) modulo \((s^*\mathcal J).a\).

1.1.3. Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a henselian local ring, with closed point \(s\). Let \(X'_o\) be a scheme of finite type over \(k(s)\), smooth outside a closed point \(x'_o\), and \(X_o\) the henselization of \(X'_o\) at \(x'_o\). We are interested in the systems
\[\xymatrix{X_o\ar[r]&X\ar[d]\\s\ar[u]\ar[r]&T\ar[r]&S}\]
of the following type
\(a\) \(T\) is local henselian essentially of finite presentation over \(S\) (I 0.0.5) ; the morphism \(T\to S\) is local and induces an isomorphism on the residue fields ;
\(b\) \(X\) is local henselian flat and essentially of finite presentation over \(T\) ;
\(c\) the special fiber of \(X/T\) is equipped with a \(k(s)\)-isomorphism with \(X_o\).
These are the henselian \(S\)-deformations of \(X_o\). A morphism from \((X,T)\) into \((X',T')\) is a commutative diagram of \(S\)-schemes
\[\xymatrix{X\ar[r]\ar[d]&X'\ar[d]\\T\ar[r]&T'}\]
inducing the identity of \(X_o\) by passage to the special fibers. A deformation \(X/T\) is said to be versal (minimally versal) if
\(a\) For every morphism \(X''/T''\to X'/T'\), such that \(T''\to T'\) is a closed embedding, the map
\[\operatorname{Hom}(X'/T',X/T)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(X''/T'',X/T)\]
is surjective. In particular (take \(X''/T''=X_o/s\)), \(\operatorname{Hom}(X'/T',X/T)\ne\varnothing\).

\(b\) For \(T'=\operatorname{Spec}(k(s)[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))\), the « inverse image » map : \(\operatorname{Hom}(T',T)\to\) (isomorphism classes of deformations of \(X_o\) over \(T'\)) is bijective.

1.1.4. Theorem (R. Elkik). There exists a versal henselian \(S\)-deformation of \(X_o\) (and only one up to non-unique isomorphism). If \(S\) is noetherian, in order that \(X/T\) be one, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) induce on the completion \(T^\wedge\) of \(T\) a versal formal \(S\)-deformation of \(X_o\) (minimally formal versal deformation, VI 1.9 and VI 4).
For the proof of this delicate theorem, we refer to R. Elkik (seminar of the ENS, 1971/72).
1.2. Ordinary quadratic singularities
Let \(y\) be a closed point of a scheme \(Y\) of finite type over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\), and let \(n\) be the dimension of \(Y\) at \(y\).

(i) For \(k\) algebraically closed, we say that \(y\) is an ordinary quadratic point of \(Y\) if the completion \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{Y,y}\) of the local ring of \(Y\) at \(y\) is isomorphic to the quotient of \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_{n+1}]]\) by the ideal generated by a single formal power series \(f\), which can be written
\[(4.2.1)\qquad f(x)=Q(x)+\text{terms of order }>2\]
with \(Q(x)\) an ordinary quadratic form.

(ii) If \(\bar k\) is an algebraic closure of \(k\), we say that \(y\) is an ordinary quadratic point of \(Y\) if the points of \(\bar Y=Y\otimes_k\bar k\) above \(y\) are ordinary quadratic points of \(\bar Y\).

1.2.2. If in this definition we replace "ordinary quadratic form" by "non-degenerate quadratic form", we obtain the notion of a non-degenerate quadratic point (VI 6.0). For \(y\) to be a non-degenerate quadratic point of \(Y\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be an ordinary quadratic point and that either \(p\ne2\), or \(n\) be odd.

Example 1.2.3. Let \(Q(x)\) be a non-degenerate quadratic form in \(n+1\) variables over \(k\). Let \(Y_o\) be the quadratic cone in \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) with equation \(Q=0\). Then the origin, which we shall denote \(y_o\), is a non-degenerate quadratic point of \(Y_o\).
Example 1.2.4. Suppose that \(p=2\) and that \(n=2m\). Let \(a\in k\), and let \(Q\) be a polynomial
\[Q(x)=(x_o^2-a)+\sum_{0<i\leq j\leq2m}a_{ij}x_ix_j.\]

We suppose that the quadratic form in (2m) variables \(\sum_{0<i\leq j}a_{ij}x_ix_j\) is non-degenerate, and we denote by \(y_o\) the closed point of \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) with coordinates \((\sqrt a,0,\ldots,0)\). Then \(y_o\) is an ordinary quadratic point of the subscheme \(Y_o\) of \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) with equation \(Q=0\).

Example 1.2.5. Let \(k'\) be a finite separable extension of \(k\) and let \(Y_o\) be a \(k'\)-scheme of the type considered in 1.2.3 (resp. 1.2.4). If we regard the scheme \(Y_o\) as a \(k\)-scheme, then \(y_o\) is a non-degenerate (resp. ordinary) quadratic point of \(Y_o\). The extension \(k'\) of \(k\) is identified with the largest separable subextension of \(k(y_o)\).

Theorem 1.2.6. Suppose that \(y\) is an ordinary quadratic point of the \(k\)-scheme \(Y\), and let \(k'\) be the largest separable subextension of \(k(y)\). There then exists a \(k'\)-scheme \(Y_o\subset\mathbb E_{k'}^{n+1}\) of type 1.2.3. or 1.2.4., and a \(k\)-isomorphism \(\varphi\) between the henselization \(Y_{(y)}\) of \(Y\) at \(y\) and the henselization \({Y_o}_{(y_o)}\) of \(Y_o\) at \(y_o\).
Let us first note that there exists an étale neighborhood \(Y'\) of \(y\) in \(Y\)
\[\xymatrix{&Y'\ar[d]\\y'\ar[ur]\ar[r]&Y}\]

which is a \(k'\)-scheme. The scheme \(Y_{(y)}\) is indeed canonically a \(k'\)-scheme. Replacing \((k,Y,y)\) by \((k',Y',y')\), this reduces us to the case where \(k=k'\), i.e. to the case where \(k(y)\) is radicial over \(k\).

The \(k(y)\)-vector space \((\Omega^1_{Y/k})_y\) is of dimension \(n+1\): it suffices to verify this after extension of scalars from \(k(y)\) to its algebraic closure, and after completion at \(y\); it is then obvious. A neighborhood of \(y\) in \(Y\) is thus a subscheme of a smooth \(k\)-scheme \(Z\) of dimension \(n+1\). Moreover, after replacing \(Y\) and \(Z\) by suitable neighborhoods of \(y\), we have that the ideal of \(\mathcal O_Z\) which defines \(Y\) is principal (same refrain as above), defined by an equation \(f\).

Lemma 1.2.7. Suppose that \(y\) is a non-degenerate quadratic point of \(Y\) and that \(k(y)\) is radicial over \(k\). Then, in a neighborhood of \(y\), \(J^n_{Y/k}\) (1.1.1) is monogenous, concentrated at \(y\) and of rank 1 over \(k\). In particular \(k(y)=k\).

It suffices to prove the first assertion after passing to the algebraic closure of \(k\), and after completion at \(y\). It is then obvious by direct calculation: with the notation of 1.2.1, it follows from Nakayama that the \(\partial f/\partial x_i\) (a fortiori \(f\) and the \(\partial f/\partial x_i\)) generate the maximal ideal.

Let us prove 1.2.6 for \(y\) non-degenerate. We may and shall assume that \(k(y)=k\), that \(Y\) is defined by an equation \(f\) in a smooth scheme \(Z\), and that there exists an étale morphism \(x:Z\to\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) from \(Z\) to \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) (a "system of local coordinates \(x_i\)") which sends \(y\) to 0. There then exists a non-degenerate quadratic form in \(n+1\) variables \(Q\) such that, at \(y\),
\[f=Q(x_i)+\text{terms of order }>2.\]
Let \(Y_o\) be defined by \(Q\) as in 4.3.
On \(Y\), the system of the \(x_i\) is an "approximate solution" of the equation \(Q(x_i)=0\): if \(\mathfrak m\) is the ideal which defines \(y\), we have
\[Q(x_i)\in\mathfrak m^3\quad(\text{on }Y).\]

Since \(Q\) is non-degenerate, the ideal of \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) generated by the \(\partial Q/\partial x_i\) is the maximal ideal at 0, and the inverse image of this ideal under \(x=(x_i):Y\to\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) is \(\mathfrak m\). By the implicit function theorem 1.1.2. (applied with \(a=\mathfrak m\) and \(\delta'=J\)) there exist on the henselization \(Y_{(y)}\) functions \(x'_i\), congruent to the \(x_i\) mod \(\mathfrak m^2\) (the \(s^*\delta'.a\) of loc. cit.), which satisfy \(Q(x'_i)=0\). They furnish the desired isomorphism between \(Y_{(y)}\) and \({Y_o}_{(y_o)}\).

Lemma 1.2.8. Suppose that \(y\) is a degenerate ordinary quadratic point (\(p=2\), \(n\) even), and that \(k(y)\) is radicial over \(k\). Then, in a neighborhood of \(y\), \(T^1_{Y/k}\) is monogenous, concentrated at \(y\) and of rank 2 over \(k\). In particular, either \(k(y)=k\), or \(k(y)\) is isomorphic to \(k(\sqrt a)\) with \(a\in k\), \(a\notin k^2\).
Let us proceed as in 1.2.7: with the notation of 1.2.1, it suffices to verify that
\[\dim_k\bigl(k[[X_1,\ldots,X_{n+1}]]/(f,\partial f/\partial X_i)\bigr)=2.\]
In a suitable coordinate system \(x_o,x_1,\ldots,x_{2m}\), the formal power series \(f\) can be written
\[f=x_o^2+\sum_1^m x_i x_{i+m}+R\qquad(\text{order }R\geq3).\]

Let then \(n'\) be the ideal generated by \(f\) and the \(\partial f/\partial x_i\), \(n\) the one generated by \(x_o^2\) and the \(x_i\;(i\ne0)\), and \(q\) the maximal ideal. Let us prove that \(n=n'\). We clearly have \(n'\subset n\), and it suffices to verify that \(n'+nq=n\) (Nakayama). Modulo \(nq\), we have \(f=x_o^2\); modulo \(nq+(x_o^2)\), we have \(\partial f/\partial x_i=x_{i+m}\) and \(\partial f/\partial x_{i+m}=x_i\) \((1\leq i\leq m)\), whence the assertion.
Under the hypotheses of 1.2.8, if \(k=k(y)\), we shall denote by \(a\) any square in \(k\).

Lemma 1.2.9. Let \(V\) be a vector bundle over a scheme \(S\), and \(T\) a subscheme of \(\mathbb E_S(V)\) which is locally free of rank 2 over \(S\). Then \(T\) is contained in one and only one line of \(\mathbb E_S(V)\).

The problem is local (fppf) on \(S\); we may thus assume that \(T/S\) admits a section. By translation, we may assume that \(T\) contains the section 0. Let \(n\) be the ideal of \(T\) which defines 0: \(n\) is locally free of rank one over \(S\). Each homogeneous linear form \(v\in V\) on \(\mathbb E_S(V)\) defines an element of \(n\), whence an epimorphism from \(V\) to \(n\). Its kernel defines the desired line.

1.2.10. In particular, if \(A\) is a radicial subscheme of rank 2 of the affine space \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic 2, there exists \(a\in k\) such that, in a suitable system of affine coordinates, \(A\) has equations \(x_o^2-a=0\), \(x_i=0\) \((1\leq i\leq n)\). It suffices to take the \(x_i\) vanishing on the line defined by \(A\).

1.2.11. Let us prove 1.2.6 for \(y\) degenerate (\(p=2\) and \(n=2m\)). We may and shall assume that \(k(y)=k(\sqrt a)\), that \(Y\) is defined by an equation \(f\) in a smooth scheme \(Z\), and that there exists a system of local coordinates at \(y\)
\[x=(x_i)_{0\leq i\leq2m}:Z\longrightarrow\mathbb E_k^{2m+1}\]
which sends \(y\) to the closed point \((\sqrt a,0,\ldots,0)\), and such that \(T^1_{Y/k}\) is isomorphic, in a neighborhood of \(y\), to \(\mathcal O_Y/(x_o^2-a,x_1,\ldots,x_{2m})\).
We then have, after possibly replacing \(f\) by a multiple
\[f=(x_o^2-a)+\sum_{1\leq i\leq j\leq2m}a_{ij}x_ix_j+R,\qquad\text{where}\]
\(a\) the form in \(2m\) variables \(\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\) is non-degenerate;
\(b\) after extension of scalars to \(\bar k\), \(R\) is of order \(\geq3\) in \(x_o-\sqrt a\) and the \(x_i\).
Let \(Y_o\) be defined by
\[Q=x_o^2-a+\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\]

On \(Y\), we may again regard the system of the \(x_i\) as an approximate solution of the equation \(Q(X_i)=0\). This time, the ideal \(\delta\) which is the inverse image under \(X\) of the ideal generated by the \(\partial Q/\partial X_i\) satisfies \(\mathcal O_Y/\delta\simeq J^n_{Y/k}\), i.e. is
\[\delta=(x_o^2-a,(x_i)_{1\leq i})\quad\text{at }y.\]
Let \(q\) be the maximal ideal which defines \(y\). To conclude as in the non-degenerate case, by an application of the implicit function theorem, it must be verified that
\[(1.2.11.1)\qquad Q(x_i)\equiv0\pmod{\delta^2q}\quad\text{on }Y.\]
Let \(\bar q\) be the ideal which defines \(y\), after extension of scalars to the algebraic closure of \(k\). One verifies that
\[\delta^2\bar q\cap\mathcal O_{Y,y}=\delta^2q,\]
so that it suffices to verify 4.11.1 after extension of scalars to \(\bar k\): we may assume that \(a=0\). At \(y\), we then have
\[\delta^2q=(x_o^5,(x_o^3x_i)_{1\leq i},(x_o^2x_ix_j)_{1\leq i,j}, (x_ix_jx_k)_{1\leq i,j,k}).\]
From this, we deduce that \(\delta^2q\supset q^5\) and that
\[x_o^2f\equiv x_o^4\pmod{\delta^2q},\quad\text{whence }\delta^2q+(x_o^2f)\supset q^4,\] \[x_of\equiv x_o^3\pmod{\delta^2q+q^4},\quad\text{whence }\delta^2q+(x_of)\supset q^3.\]
Finally, we have
\[Q(x_i)\equiv0\pmod{\delta^2q+(f)}\quad\text{on }Z,\]

Remark 1.2.12. Let \(Q\) be a not necessarily homogeneous quadratic form in \(n+1\) variables, not identically zero, and suppose that the subscheme \(Y\) of \(\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) with equation \(Q\) presents at a point \(y\) an ordinary quadratic singularity. By a linear (non-homogeneous) change of variables, we can then put \(Y\) into the form 1.2.3 or 1.2.4. In the non-degenerate case, \(k(y)=k\), and it suffices to put \(y\) at the origin of coordinates. In the degenerate case, it suffices that the support of \(T^1_{Y/k}\) be on the line \(x_i=0\) \((1\leq i\leq2m)\) (cf. 1.2.9).
1.3 Moduli of quadratic singularities
The following proposition follows from the theorem of R. Elkik 1.1.4, and from explicit calculations left to the reader (essentially contained in VI 6).

Proposition 1.3.1. Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be a local henselian scheme with closed point \(s\), and let \(Y_o\subset\mathbb E_{k(s)}^{n+1}\) be a scheme of type 1.2.3. or 1.2.4.

(i) Take \(Y_o\) of type 1.2.3, defined by the equation

\[Q(x)=\sum_{i\leq j}\bar a_{ij}x_ix_j=0\qquad(\bar a_{ij}\in k(s))\]

and let us lift the \(\bar a_{ij}\) to elements \(a_{ij}\) of \(A\). Then the versal henselian scheme over \(S\) of deformations of the singularity of \(Y_o\) at \(y_o\) is

\[T=\operatorname{Spec}(A\{b\}),\]

the versal deformation being the henselization at \(y_o\) of the \(T\)-scheme \(Y\subset\mathbb E_T^{n+1}\) with equation

\[\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j-b=0.\]

(ii) Take \(Y_o\) of type 1.2.4, defined by the equation

\[Q(x)=(x_o^2-\bar a)+\sum\bar a_{ij}x_ix_j=0 \qquad(\bar a,\bar a_{ij}\in k(s))\]

and let us lift the \(\bar a_{ij}\) and \(\bar a\) to elements \(a_{ij}\) and \(a\) of \(A\). Then the versal henselian scheme over \(S\) of deformations of the singularity of \(Y_o\) at \(y_o\) is

\[T=\operatorname{Spec}(A\{b,c\}),\]

the versal deformation being the henselization at \(y_o\) of the \(T\)-scheme \(Y\subset\mathbb E_T^{n+1}\) with equation

\[(x_o^2-a)+\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j+bx_o+c=0.\]
The following proposition follows immediately from 1.2.6 and 1.3.1.

Corollary 1.3.2. Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be a local henselian scheme with closed point \(s\) and let \(f:X\to S\) be a flat morphism of finite presentation. We suppose that at the closed point \(x\) of \(X_s\), \(X_s\) presents an ordinary quadratic singularity, that \(k(x)\) is radicial over \(k(s)\), and that \(X_s\) is of dimension \(n\).

(i) For \(x\) non-degenerate: there exist a non-degenerate quadratic form in \(n+1\) variables \(Q\), with coefficients in \(A\), and \(b\) in the maximal ideal of \(A\), such that the henselized \(S\)-scheme of \(X\) at \(x\) is isomorphic to the henselization at the origin of the subscheme of \(\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) with equation (1.3.1(i)) \(Q-b=0\).

(ii) For \(x\) degenerate (\(n=2m\), \(\operatorname{car}(k(s))=2\)): there exists a non-homogeneous quadratic form in \(n+1\) variables, with coefficients in \(A\)

\[(4.14.2)\qquad Q(X)=x_o^2+bx_o+c+\sum_{1\leq i\leq j\leq2m}a_{ij}x_ix_j,\]

such that \(b\) is in the maximal ideal, that the form in (2m) variables \(\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\) is non-degenerate, and that the henselized \(S\)-scheme of \(X\) at \(x\) is isomorphic to the henselization at \((\sqrt c,0,\ldots,0)\) of the subscheme of \(\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) with equation \(Q=0\).

Remark 1.3.3. In case (i), if the henselization \(x\) of \(X_s\) is isomorphic via an isomorphism \(\varphi_o\) to the henselization at 0 of \(X_o\subset\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\), with equation \(\sum\bar a_{ij}x_ix_j=0\), and if the \(a_{ij}\) lift the \(\bar a_{ij}\), we may take \(Q=\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\).

In case (ii), if the henselization at \(x\) of \(X_s\) is isomorphic via an isomorphism \(\varphi_o\) to the henselization at \((\sqrt a,0,\ldots,0)\) of \(X_o\subset\mathbb E_k^{n+1}\) with equation \((x_o^2+\bar a)+\sum\bar a_{ij}x_ix_j\), and if the \(a_{ij}\) lift the \(\bar a_{ij}\), we may take \(Q=x_o^2+bx_o+c+\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\), with \(c\equiv\bar a\) mod the maximal ideal.
In both cases, the isomorphism promised in 1.3.2. may be taken extending \(\varphi_o\).

Corollary 1.3.4. Let \(f:X\to S\) be as in 1.3.2. There exists a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) in \(X\) such that the points of non-smoothness of \(f\) contained in \(U\) are ordinary quadratic points of their fiber.

blocks.html: §2. Calculation of the vanishing cycles in a standard ordinary quadratic case
2.1. Preliminary: the cohomology of a cone.
2.1.1. Let \(Y\subset\mathbb P^r\) be a projective variety over an algebraically closed field \(k\). We set the following notation.

\(X\subset\mathbb A^{r+1}\) is the affine projecting cone, with origin 0;
\(X_{(o)}\) is the henselization of \(X\) at 0;
\(X^*=X-\{0\}\) and \(X^*_{(o)}=X_{(o)}-\{0\}\);
\(X_1\subset\mathbb P^{r+1}\) is the projective projecting cone of \(Y\); we have \(X=X_1-Y\);
\(\widetilde X,\widetilde X_{(o)},\widetilde X_1\) are the blow-ups of \(X,X_{(o)},X_1\) at 0;
\(Y_o\) is the inverse image of 0 in \(\widetilde X,\widetilde X_{(o)}\) or \(\widetilde X_1\);
\(h:\widetilde X_1\to Y\) is the natural projection;
\(y_o,y_\infty:Y\to\widetilde X_1\) are the sections of \(h\) with image \(Y_o\) and \(Y\);
\(F\) is an abelian torsion group whose torsion is prime to the characteristic of \(k\); all the cohomology groups considered will have coefficients in \(F\), which will not be mentioned.
Our aim in this section is to relate the cohomologies of \(X^*_{(o)}\) and \(Y\).

Proposition 2.1.2. We have
\[(i)\qquad H^i(X)\overset\sim\longrightarrow H^i(\{0\})= \begin{cases}F&\text{if }i=0,\\0&\text{if }i>0;\end{cases}\] \[(ii)\qquad H^i_{\{0\}}(X)\overset\sim\longrightarrow H_c^i(X).\]
This proposition is proved by a homotopy argument, which we shall have to formalize.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(U\) and \(V\) two schemes over \(k\), \(\Lambda\) a torsion ring with torsion prime to the characteristic of \(k\), \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}D^+(U,\Lambda)\) and \(L\in\operatorname{Ob}D^+(V,\Lambda)\).
Let us call a "morphism" \(f\) from \((U,K)\) to \((V,L)\) a pair consisting of \(f:U\to V\) and of \(\varphi:f^*L\to K\). Such a morphism induces on cohomology
\[f^*:H^*(V,L)\longrightarrow H^*(U,K).\]

Let us say that two "morphisms" \(f_o\) and \(f_1\) are homotopic if there exist a connected scheme of finite type \(T\) over \(k\), two points 0 and 1 of \(T\) and a "morphism" \(H\) from \((U\times T,\operatorname{pr}_1^*K)\) to \((V,L)\) which induces \(f_o\) and \(f_1\) on taking the fibers at 0 and 1.
Lemma 2.1.3. If \(f_o\) is homotopic to \(f_1\), then \(f_o^*\) coincides with \(f_1^*\).

We know that there exist a sequence of points \(0=x_o,x_1,\ldots,x_n=1\) of \(T\), smooth connected curves \(\Gamma_i\) and morphisms \(a_i:\Gamma_i\to T\) such that \(x_i\) and \(x_{i+1}\) are in the image of \(a_i\). To construct the \(\Gamma_i\), we normalize a connected subscheme of dimension 1 of \(T\) containing 0 and 1. This reduces us to the case where \(T\) is a smooth curve. Let
\[\xymatrix{U\times T\ar[r]^{\operatorname{pr}_1}\ar[d]_{\operatorname{pr}_2}&U\ar[d]^f\\T\ar[r]^t&k.}\]
By virtue of the theorem of base change by a smooth morphism, applied to \(t\), we have
\[t^*Rf_*K\overset\sim\longrightarrow R\operatorname{pr}_{2*}(\operatorname{pr}_1^*K).\]
The map \(f_i^*\) \((i=0,1)\) then factors as
\[H^n(V,L)\xrightarrow{H^*}H^n(U\times T,\operatorname{pr}_1^*K) \longrightarrow H^0(T,R^n\operatorname{pr}_{2*}(\operatorname{pr}_1^*K)) \longrightarrow H^n(U,K),\]
and is thus independent of \(i\).
2.1.3. Let us prove 2.1.2. The identity map of \(X\) is homotopic (thanks to the homotheties) to the map from \(X\) to \(X\) which is constant of value 0; (i) follows.
Using homotheties with ratio tending to infinity, we see that \(Y\) is a deformation retract of \(X_1^*\) and (ii) follows from the five lemma and from the diagram

\[\xymatrix{ {}\ar[r]^-\partial&{H^i_{\{0\}}(X)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^i(X_1)\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&H^i(X_1^*)\ar[r]^-\partial\ar[d]&{}\\ {}\ar[r]^-\partial&H_c^i(X)\ar[r]&H^i(X_1)\ar[r]&H^i(Y)\ar[r]^-\partial&{}}.\]

2.2. Vanishing cycles.

2.2.1. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait and \(s,\eta,\bar s,\bar\eta\) as in XIII 0.2.5, \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/k\), with \(k\) prime to the residue characteristic of \(S\), \(\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) the standard affine space of dimension \(n+1\) over \(S\) and \(X\) the closed subscheme of \(\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) defined by a quadratic equation \(Q=0\), non-zero modulo the uniformizer
\[Q(x)=\sum_{i\leq j}a_{ij}x_ix_j+\sum b_ix_i+c.\]

If \(\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) is the complement in \(\mathbb P_S^{n+1}\) of the hyperplane at infinity \(H\), then \(X\) is deduced from the quadric \(X_1\) of \(\mathbb P_S^{n+1}\) with homogeneous equation
\[(2.2.1.1)\qquad \sum_{i\leq j}a_{ij}x_ix_j+\sum b_ix_iz+cz^2=0\]
by subtracting \(Y=X_1\cap H\).
Let us make the following hypotheses.
\(a\) \(Y\) is a smooth quadric over \(S\), i.e. the quadratic form \(\sum_{i\leq j}a_{ij}x_ix_j\) is ordinary;
\(b\) the quadric \(X_s\) is singular, i.e. \(X_s\) is a quadratic cone.
We denote by \(x_o\) the singular point of \(X_s\), and by \(f\) the projection of \(X\) onto \(S\).
Remark 2.2.2. If \(x_o\) is a rational point, we can by translation put it at the origin of coordinates. If \(x_o=0\), the \(b_i\) and \(c\) are divisible by the uniformizer.

The subscheme \(A\) of \(X_s\) with equations \(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i}\) is concentrated at \(x_o\). Except in the exceptional case where \(k(s)\) is of characteristic 2 and \(n+1\) is even, this scheme is of degree one over \(k(s)\), hence \(x_o\) is rational. In the exceptional case, it is ramified over \(k(s)\) and of rank 2, so that \(k(x_o)\) is either \(k(s)\), or an inseparable quadratic extension of \(k(s)\). The assertions made about \(A\) are most easily verified after passing to the algebraic closure \(k(\bar s)\) of \(k(s)\).

Proposition 2.2.3. Under the hypotheses of 2.2.1, the morphisms
\[H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)\overset\sim\longrightarrow H^i(X_{\bar s},R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)) \overset\sim\longrightarrow R^i\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)_{x_o},\] \[H_c^i(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)\overset\sim\longleftarrow H_c^i(X_{\bar s},R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)) \overset\sim\longleftarrow H^i_{\{x_o\}}(X_{\bar s},R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda))\]
are isomorphisms.
That the left-hand arrows are isomorphisms follows from 2.1.8.6 and 2.1.10.5; that those on the right are is a corollary of 2.1.2; indeed we have a distinguished triangle
\[(\Lambda\text{ on }X_{\bar s})[0]\longrightarrow R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda) \longrightarrow R\Phi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)\]
and 2.1.2 applies to \((\Lambda\text{ on }X_{\bar s})\), while \(R\Phi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)\) is supported in \(x_o\).
Corollary 2.2.4. If the geometric generic fiber \(X_{\bar\eta}\) is singular, i.e. is again a quadratic cone, the sheaves \(R^i\Phi(\Lambda)\) are all zero.

By 2.1.2, \(\Lambda=H^0(X_{\bar s},\Lambda)\overset\sim\longrightarrow H^0(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)\), and \(0=H^i(X_{\bar s},\Lambda)=H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)\). We then apply the long exact sequence XIII, 2.1.8.9 (validated by XIII, 2.1.9).
2.2.5. Suppose now that
(*) the generic fiber \(X_{\bar\eta}\) is smooth.

To simplify, we shall also assume \(S\) strictly henselian. It is easy to calculate the sheaves of vanishing cycles with the help of 2.2.3 and XII 3.7. The cohomology with coefficients in \(\Lambda\), considered here, is deduced from the \(\ell\)-adic cohomology of loc. cit. by the universal coefficients formula (cf. 2.1.13). Here are the results.
A. Cohomology without support.
We have \(R^i\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)=0\) if \(i\ne0,n\).

1) if \(n\ne0\): we have \(\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)=\Lambda\). Moreover, \(R^n\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)\) is (non-canonically) isomorphic to the sheaf \(\Lambda\) on \(x_o\) extended by 0 over \(X_s\).

2) \(n\) arbitrary: one verifies that, even for \(n=0\), \(\Phi_{\bar\eta}^i(\Lambda)=0\) for \(i\ne n\), and that \(\Phi_{\bar\eta}^n(\Lambda)\) is (non-canonically) isomorphic to the sheaf \(\Lambda\) on \(x_o\) extended by 0 over \(X_s\).
B. Cohomology with support in \(x_o\).
We have \(H^i_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda))=0\) for \(i\ne n,2n\). We have moreover a trace morphism
\[(2.2.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{tr}:H^{2n}_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n))) \longrightarrow\Lambda.\]
if \(n\ne0\): the morphism (2.2.5.1) is an isomorphism. Moreover, \(H^n_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n)))\) is (non-canonically) isomorphic to \(\Lambda\).

C. Duality. For \(a\in R^n\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)_{x_o}\) and \(b\in H^n_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n)))\), let us set \((a,b)=\operatorname{Tr}(a\mathbin\wedge b)\), where \(\operatorname{Tr}\) is the morphism XIII 2.2.6
\[\operatorname{Tr}:H^{2n}_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n)))\longrightarrow\Lambda.\]

It follows from Poincaré duality on \(X_{\bar\eta}\) and from the isomorphisms 2.2.3 that \((a,b)\) puts the free \(\Lambda\)-modules \(R^n\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda)_{x_o}\) and \(H^n_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n)))\) in perfect duality.

By XII 3.7, the groups \(H^n_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(n)))\) and \(R^n\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m))_{x_o}\) have natural generators defined up to sign, say \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\). Let \(\varphi\) be the natural map from the first of these groups to the second. We can normalize the signs of \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\) so that
\[(2.2.5.2)\qquad (\delta',\delta)=1\qquad\text{we then have}\] \[(2.2.5.3)\qquad \varphi(\delta)=(-1)^m\cdot2\,\delta'.\]

Let \(Z\) be the separable quadratic extension of \(k(\eta)\), the center of the even part of the Clifford algebra of the quadratic form (2.2.1.1). Let \(\epsilon\) be the corresponding character of order 1 or 2 of the inertia group \(I\). For \(\sigma\in I\), we have
\[(2.2.5.4)\qquad \sigma\delta=\epsilon(\sigma)\delta\] \[(2.2.5.5)\qquad \sigma\delta'=\epsilon(\sigma)\delta'.\]
The formulas (2.2.5.2) (2.2.5.3) (2.2.5.4) in cohomology with coefficients in \(\mathbb Z/2k\) imply that the variation is
\[(2.2.5.6)\qquad \operatorname{Var}(\sigma)(a)= \frac{\epsilon(\sigma)-1}{2}(-1)^m(a\delta)\,\delta.\]
E. \(n=2m+1\) — Vanishing cycles.

By XII 3.7, the groups \(H^n_{\{x_o\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}\Lambda(m))\) and \(R^n\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m+1))_{x_o}\) have natural generators defined up to sign, say \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\). With the notation of D, we can normalize the signs of \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\) so that
\[(2.2.5.7)\qquad (\delta',\delta)=1\qquad;\quad\text{we have}\] \[(2.2.5.8)\qquad \varphi(\delta)=0.\]
F. \(n=2m+1\) — The variation.

The inertia group \(I\) acts trivially on the cohomology of \(X_{\bar\eta}\). This follows from XII 3.7 and from the fact that \(I\) acts trivially on the cohomology of \(Y_{\bar\eta}\), \(Y\) being proper and smooth over \(S\).
For \(\sigma\) in the inertia group, we necessarily have
\[(2.2.5.9)\qquad D(\sigma)(\delta')=\lambda(\sigma)\,\delta, \quad\text{with }\lambda(\sigma)\in\Lambda(1).\]
The additivity formula (XIII, 1.4.3.4) is written here
\[\lambda(\sigma\tau)=\lambda(\sigma)+\lambda(\tau).\]
Let \(e:I\longrightarrow\Lambda(1)=\mu_k\) be the homomorphism such that
\[\sigma(\sqrt[k]{t})=e(\sigma)\sqrt[k]{t}\]
for \(t\) a uniformizer. \(\lambda\) is necessarily a multiple of \(e\), in view of the structure of the tame inertia group. The formula (2.2.5.9) can be rewritten
\[(2.2.5.10)\qquad \operatorname{Var}(\sigma)(a)=\lambda_X\cdot e(\sigma)(a\delta)\delta\]
for a suitable \(\lambda_X\in\Lambda\) (depending on \(X/S\)). We shall determine \(\lambda_X\) later.
2.2.6. We shall give a description of the cycles \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\) of \(D\) and \(E\) which is local in nature for the étale topology.
The case where \(n\) is even is easy: if \(2\mid k\) (\(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/k\)), \(\pm\delta\) is characterized by (2.2.5.3) (2.2.5.4), i.e. by
\[(2.2.6.1)\qquad (\delta,\delta)=(-1)^m\cdot2.\]
In general, \(\pm\delta\) is the image of any cycle \(\widetilde\delta\), in cohomology with values in \(\mathbb Z/2^ak\) (\(a\) large), which satisfies (2.2.6.1).
Suppose \(n\) odd; \(x_o\) is then a rational point (2.2.2).

Let \(X_{s(o)}\) be the henselization at \(x_o\) of \(X_s\), \(\widetilde X_{s(o)}\) the blow-up of \(X_{s(o)}\) at \(x_o\), \(Y_o\) the inverse image of \(x_o\) in \(\widetilde X_{s(o)}\) (a quadric) and \(X^*_{s(o)}=X_{s(o)}-\{x_o\}=\widetilde X_{s(o)}-Y_o\). Let us consider the composite arrow (cf. XIII, 2.4.6.1)

\[(2.2.6.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ H^{n-1}(Y_o,\Lambda(m))&H^{n-1}(\widetilde X_{s(o)},\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-\sim\ar[r]&H^{n-1}(X^*_{s(o)},\Lambda(m))\ar[d]\\ &&H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[ll]\\ &H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,R\Phi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\ar[l]& }\]

Lemma 2.2.7. The arrow 2.2.6.2 identifies \(H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\) with the primitive quotient of the cohomology in dimension \(2m\) of the quadric \(Y_o\) of dimension \(2m\); \(\pm\delta\) is the image of the natural generators of this primitive quotient.
The cycles \(\pm\delta\) are characterized by their image in \(H_c^i(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda(m))\); using (2.1.7.1), this reduces us to showing that the composite arrow

\[\xymatrix{ H^{n-1}(Y_o,\Lambda(m))&H^{n-1}(\widetilde X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-\sim\ar[r]&H^{n-1}(X_s^*,\Lambda(m))\ar[d]\\ H_c^n(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda(m))&H_c^n(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-{\mathrm{sp}}&H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[l] }\]
sends the distinguished generators of the primitive quotient of the first group to the distinguished generators of the last group.
Let us apply 2.1.8. The arrow can be rewritten (up to sign)
\[\xymatrix{ H^{n-1}(Y_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[r]^-\partial\ar@{=}[d]&H_c^n(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[d]\\ H^{n-1}(Y_{\bar\eta},\Lambda(m))\ar[r]&H_c^n(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda(m)) }\]
and 2.2.7 follows.
§3. The Picard Lefschetz formula
3.1 Preliminary results

3.1.1. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait and \(f:X\to S\) a flat morphism of finite type, purely of relative dimension \(n\). We suppose that the special fiber \(X_s\) is smooth, except at a finite set of points \(\Sigma'\), where \(X_s\) presents an ordinary quadratic singularity.

Let \(\Sigma\) be the set of points \(x\in\Sigma'\) such that the generic fiber \(X_\eta\) is smooth in a neighborhood of \(x\). Finally, let \(\Lambda\) be a torsion ring, prime to the residue characteristic of \(S\).

Proposition 3.1.2. (i) The sheaves \(R^i\Phi(\Lambda)\) are zero for \(i\ne n\).
(ii) The sheaf \(R^n\Phi(\Lambda)\) is zero outside \(\Sigma\). Its restriction to \(\Sigma\) is a sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules of rank 1.
(iii) For every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(\Sigma\), the form \((a,b)\) \(2.2.5 (C)\) puts the \(\Lambda\)-modules \((R^n\psi(\Lambda))_{\bar x}\) and \(H^n_{\{\bar x\}}(R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda))\) in duality. These are free of rank one for \(n\ne0\), of rank 2 for \(n=0\).

The assertions of 3.1.2 are local in nature for the étale topology. This allows us to treat only the case where \(S\) is strictly henselian, and where \(X\) is the hypersurface of the relative affine space \(\mathbb A_S^{n+1}\) defined by a quadratic equation satisfying 2.2.1 \(a\) \(b\) (apply 1.3.2).

\(f\) is smooth at every point of \(X_s-\Sigma'\), so that \(R\Phi=0\) outside \(\Sigma'\). By 2.2.4, \(R\Phi\) is even zero outside \(\Sigma\). We may now assume 2.2.5 (*). The assertions (ii) and (iii) then follow from 2.2.5 \(A\) \(B\) \(C\).
3.2. Even relative dimension.
We keep the notation of 3.1, taking for \(n\) an even integer \(2m\). To simplify, we assume \(S\) strictly henselian (the general case would be obtained by descent).

Proposition 3.2.1. (i) For \(x\in\Sigma\), the group \(H^n_{\{x\}}(R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\) has a natural generator \(\delta\), well defined up to sign, characterized by the conditions of being natural in \(\Lambda\) and of satisfying
\[(\delta,\delta)=(-1)^m2\]
(for \(n=0\), add the condition \(\operatorname{Tr}(\delta)=0\)).
(ii) For a suitable character \(\epsilon_x:I\to\{\pm1\}\) (independent of \(\Lambda\)) of the inertia group \(I\), the variation is written
\[\operatorname{Var}(\sigma)(a)=(-1)^m\frac{\epsilon_x(\sigma)-1}{2}(a\delta)\delta\]
(for \(a\in R^n\Phi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m))\)). It follows that
\[\sigma(\delta)=\epsilon_x(\sigma)\cdot\delta.\]

Let us pass to the limit so as to treat only the universal case where \(\Lambda\) would be \(\mathbb Z_\ell\). Up to sign, only one \(\delta\) can then satisfy the conditions of (i). As in 3.1.2, we then reduce to the case 2.2.5 (*), and we apply 2.2.5 \(D\).
Complement 3.2.2. If, at \(x\in\Sigma\), the henselization \(X_{(x)}\) of \(X\) at \(x\) is isomorphic to the henselization at \(x_o\) of the projective scheme \(Q\) with equation,
\[\sum_{i\le j}a_{ij}x_ix_j=0,\]
then the character \(\epsilon_x\) is defined by the separable quadratic extension \(Z(C^+(Q))\) of the field of fractions (this follows from 2.2.5 \(D\)).
3.2.3. In residue characteristic \(\ne2\), we can make 3.2.2 more explicit. In this case, \(I\) admits a single non-trivial character \(\epsilon\) of order 2. For \(t\) a uniformizer, we have
\[\sigma(\sqrt t)=\epsilon(\sigma)\sqrt t.\]
On the other hand, \(X_{(x)}\) is then isomorphic to the henselization at 0 of an affine scheme
\[\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j=b,\]

where \(b\) is in the maximal ideal and \(Q(x)=\sum a_{ij}x_ix_j\) is non-degenerate. The ideal \((b)\) defines the image of the subscheme of \(X_{(x)}\), the support of \(J^n_{X_{(x)}/S}\). The center of the Clifford algebra is isomorphic to \(k(\eta)(((-1)^{m+1}\cdot2b\det(a_{ij}))^{1/2})\) (Bourbaki, Alg. Ch 9, §9, n° 4 final Rmk), whence
\[\epsilon_x=\epsilon^{v(b)}.\]
The variation is zero if \(b\) is of even valuation, and otherwise \(\epsilon_x=\epsilon\).
3.3. Odd relative dimension

To prove the Picard-Lefschetz formula in odd relative dimension, we shall have to reduce to the transcendental case, treated in XIV 3.2.1.1. The specialization argument required forces us to work over a base which is no longer of dimension one. We shall do this by ad hoc arguments, adapted to the precise case we need: I do not have at my disposal a serviceable theory over a base of dimension > 1, and I have preferred not to cover up my ignorance with generalities.

3.3.1. Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be a strictly local noetherian scheme and \(f:X\to S\) a flat morphism of finite type, purely of relative dimension \(n\), whose special fiber \(X_s\) has as its only points of non-smoothness non-degenerate quadratic points. Let \(s\) be the closed point of \(S\), \(X_s\) the special fiber and \(x_o\in X_s\) a singular point. By 1.3.2 (i), in a neighborhood of \(x_o\), \(X/S\) is locally isomorphic to an \(S\)-scheme defined by an equation \(Q-b=0\) in \(\mathbb A_S^{n+1}\), with \(b\) in the maximal ideal and \(Q\) a non-degenerate quadratic form. The ideal \((b)\) of \(A\) depends only on \((X/S,x_o)\): in a neighborhood of \(x\), there exists a section \(s\in X(A/(b))\) of \(X\) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(A/(b))\) such that \(J^n_{X/S}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{s(\operatorname{Spec}(A/(b)))}\). We denote by \(s_x\) this section and by \(b(x)\) an arbitrary generator of \((b)\).

3.3.2. Suppose \(S\) normal, and let \(b\in A\). For \(n\) invertible in \(A\) and \(b\ne0\), we shall denote by \(e_b^{(n)}\) the following homomorphism from the Galois group of the field of fractions of \(A\) to \(\mathbb Z/(n)(1)\)
\[e_b^{(n)}(\sigma)=(\sigma\sqrt[n]{b})/\sqrt[n]{b}.\]

We have \((e_b^{(nm)})^m=e_b^{(n)}\). If \(\Lambda\) is a \(\mathbb Z/n\)-algebra, we again denote by \(e_b\) the composite \[\operatorname{Gal}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z/(n)(1)\longrightarrow\Lambda(1).\] It is independent of \(n\).
The character \(e_b\) factors through \(\pi_1(S-(\text{subscheme }b=0))\).

3.3.3. Suppose that \(S\) is a strictly henselian trait, and that \(n\) is odd: \(n=2m+1\). Let \(x\) be a singular point of \(X_s\) in a neighborhood of which \(X_\eta\) is smooth, and \(X_1/S\) a subscheme with equation \(Q-b=0\) of \(\mathbb A_S^{n+1}\) as in 1.3.2 (i). The hypothesis on \(x\) means that \(b\), zero modulo the maximal ideal, is not 0.

Let \(X_{s(x)}\) be the henselization of \(X_s\) at \(x\), \(\widetilde X_{s(x)}\) the blow-up of \(\widetilde X_s\) at \(x\), \(Y\) the quadric inverse image of \(x\) and \(\widetilde X^*_{s(x)}=\widetilde X_{s(x)}-[x]=\widetilde X_{s(x)}-Y\). The composite morphism

\[\xymatrix{ H^{n-1}(Y,\Lambda(m))&H^{n-1}(\widetilde X_{s(x)},\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-\sim\ar[r]&H^{n-1}(X^*_{s(x)},\Lambda(m))\ar[d]\\ &&H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[ll]\\ &H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\ar[l]& }\]

identifies this last group with the primitive quotient (XII 3.5) of the cohomology of the quadric \(Y\). By (2.2.7), this is indeed the case in a locally isomorphic situation. The images of the two natural generators of this quotient (cohomology classes of the generating lines of \(Y\)) are two basis vectors \(\pm\delta\) of \(H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\).

3.3.4. Let us return to the more general hypotheses of 3.3.1, with \(n=2m+1\). For every singular point \(x\) of \(X_s\), we shall again denote by \(\pm\delta_x\) the sections of \(R^nf_!\Lambda(m)\) over \(S\) which are the image of the generators of the primitive quotient of \(H^{n-1}(Y,\Lambda(m))\) (same notation as above) under the composite morphism

\[\xymatrix{ H^{n-1}(Y,\Lambda(m))&H^{n-1}(\widetilde X_{s(x)},\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-\sim\ar[r]&H^{n-1}(X^*_{s(x)},\Lambda(m))\ar[d]\\ H^0(S,R^nf_!\Lambda(m))&H_c^n(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[l]_-\sim&H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,\Lambda(m))\ar[l] }\]
\(\pm\delta_x\) is the vanishing cycle at \(x\).
When \(S\) is a trait, \(\pm\delta\in H_c^n(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda(m))\) is the image under XIII 2.1.8.4 of the class (3.3.3) \(\pm\delta\in H^n_{\{x\}}(X_s,R\psi_{\bar\eta}(\Lambda(m)))\).
We shall prove the two following results simultaneously.

Proposition 3.3.5. For \(S\) a henselian trait and \(X/S\) as in 3.3.3, the variation is given by
\[\operatorname{Var}(\sigma)(a)=(-1)^{m+1}e_{b(x)}(\sigma)(a\delta)\delta.\]

Proposition 3.3.6. Let \(S\) be strictly local regular and \(f:X\to S\) a proper flat morphism of relative dimension \(n=2m+1\) such that \(X_s\) presents one and only one point of non-smoothness \(x\) — assumed quadratic non-degenerate. We also suppose that \(b(x)\) is a parameter. Then Galois acts on the cohomology of the geometric generic fiber by
\[\sigma(a)=a+(-1)^{m+1}e_{b(x)}(\sigma)(a\delta_x)\delta_x.\]
\(A\) Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic 0, \(S\) the henselization of \(\operatorname{Spec}(k[T])\) at 0. Let us prove 3.3.5 when \(b(x)\) is a uniformizer.

By the Lefschetz principle, we may reduce to assuming that \(k=\mathbb C\). The question being local, we may assume that \(f:X\to S\) is deduced by base change from the map \(\sum z_i^2\) from \(\mathbb C^{n+1}\) to \(\mathbb C\). The construction given of \(\pm\delta\) has a transcendental analogue, in integral cohomology. Via XIV 2.1, \(\pm\delta\) thus corresponds to a transcendental vanishing cycle, the image of
\[\pm\delta_{\mathbb C\ell}\in H^n_{\{0\}}(X_o,R\psi_{\mathbb C\ell\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z)).\]

By XIV 2.1 and the corresponding property of the algebraic cycles \(\pm\delta\), the image of \(\pm\delta_{\mathbb C\ell}\) in \(H^n_{\{0\}}(X_o,R\psi_{\mathbb C\ell\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z))\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell\) is a generator of this group. From then on, \(\pm\delta_{\mathbb C\ell}\) are two generators of \(H^n_{\{0\}}(X_o,R\psi_{\mathbb C\ell\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z))\), and coincide with the vanishing cycles of XIV 3.2. The formula 3.3.5 thus follows from XIV 3.2.11 (in all rigor, one would have to have verified a compatibility between the isomorphism XIV 2.1 and the cup-products and traces).
\(B\) Let us prove 3.3.6 for \(X/S\) as in \(A\) (and moreover proper).
This follows from XIII 2.4.6.2.
\(C\) Let \(p\) be a prime number and \(S_o\) the strict henselization of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z[T])\) at \((p,T)\). Let us prove 3.3.6 for \(X/S_o\) such that \(b(x)=T\).

The restriction of the sheaf \(R^if_*\Lambda\) to the strict henselization of \(S\) at \((T)\) is amenable to \(B\). This sheaf being locally constant on the complement of the smooth divisor \(T=0\), which is of unequal characteristic, \(C\) follows from \(B\) and from Abhyankar's lemma.
\(D\) In at least one case where \(C\) applies, \(\delta_x\) is not a torsion element in \(\ell\)-adic cohomology.

Let \(S_1\) be the strict henselization of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\) at \((p)\) and \(V\subset\mathbb P^r/S_1\) a smooth projective variety over \(S_1\), purely of relative dimension \(n+1\). We suppose that there exists a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections for the special fiber \(V_p\) of \(V/S_1\) (XVII, 2.5); this pencil is defined by a line \(d'\) in the special fiber of the projective space \(\check{\mathbb P}^r/S_1\) dual to \(\mathbb P^r/S_1\). Let us lift \(d'\) to a line \(d\) of \(\check{\mathbb P}^r/S_1\) and let \(X\to d\) be the pencil of hyperplane sections of \(V\) parametrized by \(d\) (XVIII 3.1). The exceptional locus \(\Delta\) of \(d\) is étale over \(S_1\) (XVII 6). By étale localization at a point of \(\Delta_p\subset d\), we thus obtain a situation \(C\).

To verify that \(\delta_x\) is not torsion, it suffices to do so after extension of scalars from \(S_1\) to \(\mathbb C\). Let us take \(V=\mathbb P^{n+1}\), and the projective embedding defined by \(\mathcal O(N)\). For \(N\ge2\), we can apply XVII 2.5 (valid for every \(N\) in this particular case). We then apply the following lemma.
Lemma 3.3.7. For a Lefschetz pencil of hypersurfaces of large degree \(N\) in \(\mathbb P^{n+1}(\mathbb C)\) (\(N\ge3\) suffices), a vanishing cycle (in \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomology) is non-zero.

This lemma follows from the theory of Lefschetz pencils, for example from XVIII 6.6.1. There is no vicious circle in using here this result of XVIII, based on the "Picard-Lefschetz formula", for we already have \(A\) at our disposal. One can also use the more direct variant XIX n° 4, which is purely transcendental and depends only on XIV 3.2.
\(E\) Proof of 3.3.5 in residue characteristic \(p\) for \((X/S,x\in X_s)\).

Let \(X_o/S_o\) be as in \(D\). By 1.3.2 (i), there exists \(g:S\to S_o\) such that the henselization at \(x_o\) of \(g^*X_o=X_o\times_{S_o}S\) is \(S\)-isomorphic to the henselization of \(X\) at \(x\). It thus suffices to prove 3.3.5 for \(g^*X_o\). We deduce by base change from \(C\) for \(X_o/S_o\) that
\[\sigma(a)=a-(-1)^m e_{b(x)}(\sigma)(a\delta_x)\delta_x\]
in \(H^n(X_{o\bar\eta})\), for \(\sigma\) in the inertia group. Let \(\pi\) be a uniformizer, and let us compare the formula above with (2.2.5.9), where \(e=e_\pi\). We find that
\[\lambda_X e_\pi(\sigma)(a\delta_x)\delta_x=-(-1)^m e_{b(x)}(\sigma)(a\delta_x)\delta_x\]

in \(H^n(X_{o\bar\eta})\). Since \(\delta_x\) is not torsion, we can cancel \((a\delta_x)\delta_x\) from this formula; the formula 2.2.5.9, for the value of \(\lambda_X\) obtained, is none other than 3.3.5.
\(F\) Proof of 3.3.6 (general case)
3.3.6 is deduced from the general case of 3.3.5 as \(B\) and \(C\) from \(A\).

The propositions 3.1.2, 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.2.3 and 3.3.5 constitute the "Picard-Lefschetz formula". This formula is the algebraic analogue of XIV 3.2.11, but takes a very different form according to the parity of the relative dimension.
In the most interesting case where \(X/S\) is proper, the results can be summarized as follows.

Theorem 3.4 (Picard-Lefschetz formula). Let \(S\) be a strictly henselian trait and \(f:X\to S\) a proper flat morphism purely of relative dimension \(n=2m\) or \(2m+1\). We suppose that \(X_\eta\) is smooth and that \(X_s\) presents only ordinary quadratic singularities. Let \(\Sigma\) be the singular locus of \(X_s\).

(i) To each \(x\in\Sigma\) is attached a vanishing cycle \(\pm\delta_x\in H^n(X_{\bar\eta})(m)\). These \(\delta_x\) are pairwise orthogonal. For \(n\) even, we have \((\delta_x,\delta_x)=(-1)^m2\).

(ii) We have \(H^i(X_s)\overset\sim\longrightarrow H^i(X_{\bar\eta})\) for \(i\ne n,n+1\) and a long exact sequence

\[ 0\longrightarrow H^n(X_s)\longrightarrow H^n(X_{\bar\eta}) \xrightarrow{((\ ,\delta_x))}\bigoplus_{x\in\Sigma}\Lambda(n-m) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(X_s)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(X_{\bar\eta})\longrightarrow0. \]

(iii) Local characters of the inertia group are defined:

\[ \begin{array}{lll} n\ \text{even} & \varepsilon_x:I\longrightarrow\{\pm1\},\\[4pt] n\ \text{odd} & e_x:I\longrightarrow\Lambda(1). \end{array} \]

For \(X\) regular at \(x\), they are surjective; for \(x\) non-degenerate, they are the unique character of order 2 and the canonical character.

The action of the inertia group \(I\) is furnished by

\[ \begin{array}{lll} n=2m & \displaystyle \sigma(a)=a+(-1)^m\sum_{x\in\Sigma} \frac{\varepsilon_x(\sigma)-1}{2}(a\delta_x)\delta_x,\\[8pt] n=2m+1 & \displaystyle \sigma(a)=a-(-1)^m\sum_{x\in\Sigma} e_x(\sigma)(a\delta_x)\delta_x. \end{array} \]

In particular

\[ \begin{array}{lll} n\ \text{even} & \sigma(\delta_x)=\varepsilon_x(\sigma)\delta_x,\\[4pt] n\ \text{odd} & \sigma(\delta_x)=\delta_x. \end{array} \]
